title: practical english usage - Part 192
content:
depends on another.

We sometimes construct sentences with if... then to emphasise that one thing 9 if.  .. then married soon.) I wouldn't be surprised if she didn't get married soon. (= ... if she got should ask ...) I wonder if we shouldn't ask the doctor to look at Emily. (= I wonder if we or uncertainty.

An extra not is sometimes put into if clauses after expressions suggesting doubt 8  extra    negative:      I wonder         if  we    shouldn't        ...

I might see you tomorrow. If not, then it'll be Saturday. (= ... If I don’ t see Are you free? If so, let's go out for a meal. (= ... If you are free . ..) After if, we  can use so and not instead of repeating a whole clause.

7 if  so   and   if  not For more details of ellipsis (structures with words left out), ►    275-280.

If about to go on a long journey, try to have a good night's sleep.

If in doubt, ask for help. (= If you are in doubt...) He seldom if ever travels abroad.

I'm not angry. If anything I feel a little surprised.

There is little if any good evidence for flying saucers.

I'll work late tonight if necessary. (= ... if it is necessary) expressions if necessary, if any, if anything, if ever, if in doubt.

We sometimes leave out subject + be after if. Note the common fixed 6 leaving      out   words     after    if For other uses of inverted word order, ►    270-271.

(xiu Hmbt't tw missed...) Had we not missed the plane, we would all have been killed in the crash.

Negatives are not contracted.

Should you change your mind,... (= If you should change ...) Had I realised what you intended, ... (= If I had realised ...) Were she my daughter,... (= If she were my  daughter ...) the subject. This happens mostly with were, had and should.

In formal and literary styles, if can be dropped and an auxiliary verb put before 5  leaving      out   if:  formal     inversion       structures        - Had   I realised        ...

grammar •  245  other structures found in spoken English of would have (they are pronounced the same: /'wodəv/  in normal speech).

Confusion about this structure sometimes leads people to write would of instead was happening.

If you wouldn't have phoned her, we'd never have found out what If I would've had a gun,  somebody might have got hurt.

We   would never have met if he hadn't have crashed into my car.

I didn't know. But if I had've known ...

genuine examples taken from conversation.

for emphasis or in negatives. Both had and would occur. The  following are Instead of the contracted ’ d  in these structures, full forms are sometimes used 3 had've       and   would've would have gone on and won that second set. (from a tennis commentary) You wonder what the attitudes of both players would have been if Nadal It would have been funny if she'd have recognised him.

If I'd have known, I'd have told you.

in speech. It is not usually written, and is incorrect in formal writing.

constructed with 'd have. This, too, is very informal, but it occurs quite often In informal spoken English, if-c lauses referring to the past are sometimes 2  'd  have     .. . 'd  have;    would       have      ...  would       have For if. . . would in polite requests. ►    243.4.

How would we feel if this would happen to our family?

It would be good if we'd get some rain.

incorrect in formal writing.

very informal, and quite common in speech. It is not usually written, and is Conditional would is sometimes used in both clauses of an if-sentence. This is 1  would       in  both    clauses 245 other     structures       found     in  spoken      English His style may be simple, but it is pleasant to read.

The  same kind of idea can be expressed with may. .. but.

The profits, if a little lower than last year's, are still extremely healthy.

His style, if simple, is pleasant to read.

not very important.

although; it can suggest that what is being talked about is a matter of opinion, or common in the structure if + adjective (with no verb). If is not as definite as In a formal style, if can be used with a similar meaning to although. This is 13 if  meaning       'although' If you want to go home, Anne's got your car keys.
